---
title: "Testing Bash, Python, and R"
subtitle: "Testing training — Module 03"
lang: en
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    code-block-height: 400px
---

## 🇫🇷

[🇫🇷 Version française](../../slides/03_bash_python_r.qmd)

---

## Goal

Apply the same testing logic in three different languages,
on a common example: `records.tsv`.

. . .

This module is not a framework tutorial.
To master the full syntax → **RTFM** (links in the exercises).

---

## Why test scripts outside Nextflow?

The scripts `validate_records.sh`, `parse_records.py`, `stats_records.R`:

- are called by pipeline processes
- can fail silently (i.e. poor error handling)
- can be reused across multiple projects

. . .

**A bug in a script = a bug in the pipeline.**

. . .

Why *outside* Nextflow? Immediate feedback, no Nextflow overhead, targeted debugging.

---

## The scripts we will test {.smaller}

| Script | Input | What it does |
|--------|-------|--------------|
| `validate_records.sh` | TSV file | checks existence, header, numeric columns → exit 0/1 |
| `parse_records.py` | TSV file | loads, validates types and id uniqueness → DataFrame or exception |
| `stats_records.R` | DataFrame | computes mean and sd per group → data.frame |

These three scripts are called by the pipeline. Testing their contract = testing the expected behaviour.

---

## The common dataset {.smaller}

`records.tsv` — 20 valid rows · columns: id, group, value · **reference data (happy path)**

Each error file violates a single rule:

| File | Violated rule |
|------|--------------|
| `empty.tsv` | no data |
| `invalid_records.tsv` | non-numeric value |
| `duplicated_records.tsv` | non-unique id |
| `missing.tsv` | missing file |

One file = one violated rule = one diagnostic test.

---

## bats — minimal syntax

```bash
@test "test name" {
    run bash script.sh arg    # runs the command, captures status + output
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]       # exit code
    [[ "$output" =~ "text" ]] # standard output content
}
```

Documentation: <https://bats-core.readthedocs.io>

---

## bats — Bash

```bash
DATA="training/data"

@test "valid file → exit 0 and message with count" {
    run bash validate_records.sh "$DATA/records.tsv"
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]                     # exit code
    [[ "$output" =~ "20 records" ]]         # content — not just the code
}

@test "non-numeric value → exit 1 and error message" {
    run bash validate_records.sh "$DATA/invalid_records.tsv"
    [ "$status" -eq 1 ]
    [[ "$output" =~ "ERROR" ]]              # check the explicit message
}
```

---

## bats — testing a container

```bash
@test "correct version" {
    run apptainer exec fastqc_0.12.1.sif fastqc --version
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
    [[ "$output" =~ "FastQC v0.12.1" ]]
}

@test "tool accessible without /home mounted" {
    run apptainer exec --no-home fastqc_0.12.1.sif fastqc --version
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
}
```

Same logic as testing a script — GIVEN (the image), WHEN (the command), THEN (version + exit code).

::: {style="font-size:0.55em; color:#666; margin-top:0.5em;"}
→ [Container policy](../../container-policy.qmd) — exact tag, `.def` versioned, `.sif` not committed
:::

---

## pytest — minimal syntax

```python
def test_name():
    assert expr                                       # simple check
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="message"):  # expected exception
        function()

@pytest.mark.parametrize("path,exc", [
    ("a.tsv", ValueError),
    ("b.tsv", FileNotFoundError),
])
def test_multi(path, exc):
    with pytest.raises(exc): function(path)           # N cases, 1 test
```

Documentation: <https://docs.pytest.org>

---

## pytest — Python

```python
def test_valid_file():                              # happy path
    df = load_records(DATA / "records.tsv")
    assert df.shape == (20, 3)
    assert list(df.columns) == ["id", "group", "value"]

@pytest.mark.parametrize("path,exc", [
    ("empty.tsv",   ValueError),
    ("missing.tsv", FileNotFoundError),
])
def test_invalid_inputs(path, exc):
    with pytest.raises(exc):                            # N cases, 1 test
        load_records(DATA / path)
```

---

## testthat — minimal syntax

```r
test_that("test name", {
    expect_equal(a, b)             # equality
    expect_true(all(x >= 0))       # invariant
    expect_error(f(), "pattern")   # expected error
    expect_named(df, c("a", "b"))  # column names
})
```

Documentation: <https://testthat.r-lib.org>

---

## testthat — R

```r
test_that("missing column → explicit error", {
    bad <- data.frame(id="S01", group="A")              # in-memory data — GIVEN
    expect_error(compute_stats(bad), "Missing columns") # WHEN + THEN
})

test_that("result has correct columns and an invariant", {
    df <- data.frame(                                   # in-memory data — GIVEN
        id = c("S01","S02"), group = c("A","B"), value = c(10, 20))
    result <- compute_stats(df)
    expect_named(result, c("group", "mean", "sd"))      # exact structure
    expect_true(all(result$mean >= 0))                  # business invariant
})
```

---

## Key takeaway

::: {.callout-important}
**Every script called by a workflow must be testable outside the workflow.**
If it cannot be tested on its own, the script does too many things.

The syntax changes. The logic does not:
test business invariants (`>= 0`, expected columns) as much as behaviour.
:::

---

## Exercises → Modules 01, 02, 03

- `exercises/01_bash/` — bats on `validate_records.sh`
- `exercises/02_python/` — pytest on `parse_records.py`
- `exercises/03_r/` — testthat on `stats_records.R`
